name: Darién Gap Migration Corridor 2023-2024
description: A journalism notebook showing the route from Colombia into Panama through the Darién Gap, the reception sites that absorb arrivals, and the seasonal pressures on the corridor.

sides.name: Migratory movement
sides.description: The corridor is shaped by the movement of people, but also by the sequence of places that determine what the crossing actually feels like.

subUnits.name: Northbound migrant flow
subUnits.description:
The population moving through the Darién is not a single group, but the corridor itself imposes a common geography of risk and delay.
- Role: Human movement through the corridor
- Geographic anchor: Runs from embarkation points near Necoclí and Capurganá through the jungle to Bajo Chiquito and onward to reception sites in Panama.
- Why it matters: Everything else in the notebook is a response to or exploitation of this movement.
- What to watch: Seasonality, family movement, injuries, and whether onward transport can absorb arrivals.

subUnits.name: Route brokers and smuggling guides
subUnits.description:
Smuggling and guide networks shape the practical route, the cost of transit, and the exposure of migrants to theft, extortion, and misinformation.
- Role: Informal corridor management
- Geographic anchor: Concentrated around embarkation zones and the first jungle segments where route knowledge is most valuable.
- Why it matters: These actors influence flow volume and route selection even when they remain only partially visible.
- What to watch: Pricing, route shifts, and reports of violence or coercion on the trail.

sides.name: Response and reception
sides.description: Panamanian authorities and humanitarian organizations transform the route from a jungle crossing into a camp, transport, and protection problem.

subUnits.name: Panamanian reception network
subUnits.description:
The reception system in eastern Panama handles registration, temporary shelter, and the first organized services after the crossing.
- Role: Reception and onward movement management
- Geographic anchor: Centered on Bajo Chiquito, Lajas Blancas, and onward transport nodes deeper inside Panama.
- Why it matters: It is the first place where state capacity becomes visible after the jungle crossing.
- What to watch: Camp capacity, sanitation, onward transport speed, and child protection demand.

subUnits.name: Humanitarian field teams
subUnits.description:
UN and NGO responders work the corridor as a protection and public health emergency layered onto a border-management challenge.
- Role: Protection, medical, and child-focused response
- Geographic anchor: Most visible at reception sites and the first settlements reached after the crossing.
- Why it matters: They reveal how quickly the route produces dehydration, injury, family separation, and protection incidents.
- What to watch: Medical caseloads, vulnerable children, and how long arrivals remain in precarious conditions before onward movement.

events.title: Crossings accelerate above prior-year pace
events.description: The corridor entered 2023 with higher baseline movement, setting the stage for a year in which previous annual records would be overtaken.

events.title: Seasonal conditions compound route risks
events.description: Heavy rain and terrain stress made the route even more dangerous while reception systems continued to absorb high numbers of arrivals.

events.title: Annual crossing count surpasses the 2022 record
events.description: By early autumn, the route had already exceeded the previous year's total, confirming that the movement was no longer episodic but structurally large.

events.title: Crossings top half a million for the year
events.description: The corridor closed 2023 as one of the hemisphere's defining migration routes, with more than 500,000 crossings recorded.

events.title: New control measures alter the corridor conversation
events.description: Panama and regional governments explored ways to reduce or redirect the flow, adding a policy layer to what had previously been read mostly as a humanitarian and reporting story.

events.title: Reception bottlenecks remain the key operational question
events.description: Even as route conditions and policy choices changed, the practical test remained whether eastern Panama's reception system could process arrivals safely and quickly.

meta.name: Necoclí embarkation zone
meta.description: One of the main Colombian-side departure areas feeding the corridor toward the Darién crossing.

meta.name: Capurganá and coastal launch area
meta.description: Coastal settlement and staging point from which migrants and guides move toward the interior crossing routes.

meta.name: Darién jungle route
meta.description: Simplified line of the jungle segment between the Colombian coastal departure area and the first Panamanian settlements.

meta.name: Bajo Chiquito
meta.description: One of the first villages reached in Panama after the crossing and a key point for immediate triage and aid.

meta.name: Lajas Blancas reception site
meta.description: Panama's major reception zone for arrivals emerging from the Darién route.
